critic_raw:
This multi-spectral CNN for algal biomass prediction has several fundamental methodological problems.

1. IN-SITU LABEL COLLECTION WITHIN +/-2 DAYS OF IMAGE ACQUISITION INTRODUCES SUBSTANTIAL TEMPORAL MISMATCH FOR DYNAMIC BLOOMS. Algal blooms are dynamic -- biomass can double or halve within 24-48 hours under favorable conditions. A label collected 2 days after image acquisition may reflect a substantially different biomass state than what the image captures. The design excludes samples with more than 2-day lag, but retains all within-2-day samples without distinguishing same-day from 2-day lag. This temporal mismatch is not a random error -- it is systematic in the direction of underestimating label accuracy, and blooms that are growing rapidly (the most ecologically important events) will have the largest mismatch between image-time biomass and label-time biomass.

2. SPATIAL PATCH EXTRACTION IS CENTERED ON THE SAMPLING LOCATION OR LARGEST BLOOM REGION -- THESE ARE DIFFERENT STRATEGIES. The design says patches are centered on either the sampling location or the largest bloom region. These are distinct spatial reference strategies: centering on the sampling location produces a patch that corresponds to where biomass was measured; centering on the largest bloom region produces a patch optimized for spectral signal strength but not co-located with the label. Using the largest bloom region as the patch center for some images but the sampling location for others introduces a systematic inconsistency in what the CNN is learning to predict -- in some cases, peak bloom spectral signature vs. label at the sampling location; in others, spatially matched signal and label.

3. BY-YEAR TEMPORAL SPLIT PRESERVES ALL LAKES IN EACH SPLIT BUT DOES NOT PREVENT LAKE-SPECIFIC BLOOM SEASONALITY FROM INFLATING PERFORMANCE. The split is by year (years 1-3 train, year 4 val, year 5 test) with all lakes present in each split. Bloom timing is lake-specific and partly driven by fixed characteristics (depth, nutrient loading, watershed hydrology). If a particular lake consistently blooms in July and the June and August images show no bloom, the model can learn the lake identity as a feature (from site-specific spectral characteristics) and use the temporal encoding (time-of-year features) to predict bloom presence without learning general spectral biomass signatures. The by-lake-within-year evaluation does not prevent this lake-identity shortcut.

4. NO HAND-CRAFTED SPECTRAL INDICES (NDVI, NDCI) IN BASELINE IS A CHOICE THAT DISADVANTAGES THE BASELINE RELATIVE TO DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE. The design explicitly excludes hand-crafted spectral indices from both CNN and baseline, stating that the CNN learns spectral relationships from raw bands. But well-established spectral indices (NDCI, FAI, CHL index) are proven biomass proxies with decades of validation in remote sensing of algal blooms. Excluding them from the baseline means the RGB-only baseline is compared to a multi-spectral CNN that has access to the NIR and SWIR bands from which these indices are derived. If the multi-spectral CNN is simply learning a spectral index, the comparison does not establish that CNNs add value over classical remote sensing -- it establishes that having the right spectral bands matters, which was already known.

5. MODEL RETRAINING ON TRAIN+VALIDATION IS UNSPECIFIED BUT CONSEQUENTIAL. The design describes a standard train-val-test setup but the model selection section selects the best hyperparameter configuration on validation and is silent on whether the final model is retrained on train+validation before test evaluation. If not retrained, the model is evaluated on year 5 test data with a model trained only on years 1-3, having never seen year 4. If retrained on years 1-4, the model benefits from additional data at test time. This decision has non-trivial effects on test performance and must be explicitly stated.
all_issues_raised: Up to 2-day temporal mismatch between image acquisition and in-situ label collection is asymmetric in rapidly evolving blooms
Spatial patch centered on sampling location vs. largest bloom region introduces inconsistent correspondence between CNN input and label location
Lake identity can serve as a shortcut feature in combination with time-of-year encoding, allowing bloom prediction without learning spectral biomass signatures
Excluding hand-crafted spectral indices from the baseline understates the value of multi-spectral bands relative to classical remote sensing knowledge
Whether the final model is retrained on train+validation before test evaluation is unspecified but consequential for result interpretation
Spatial autocorrelation between training and test patches from same lakes not addressed; by-year split does not prevent lake-specific spectral overfitting
In-situ point samples may not correspond to heterogeneous bloom patterns within satellite patch spatial extent
Atmospheric correction fitted on training data rather than using physically derived per-scene parameters; training-set correction applied to test images introduces systematic spectral errors
Multi-spectral model has 7 additional information channels versus RGB baseline; comparison conflates information advantage with spectral overfitting risk
Claimed 3,000-5,000 image-label pairs implies non-independent spatial sub-sampling from same acquisitions; patch independence assumption violated
Year-5 satellite imagery may be processed with updated atmospheric correction algorithms that change multi-spectral band relationships learned from years 1-3
Spatial patch size introduces systematic mismatch between in-situ point measurements and CNN spatial integration in heterogeneous bloom conditions
CNN learning spectral relationships from 3,000-5,000 training pairs may be insufficient to rediscover physics-based spectral indices, favoring dataset artifacts over generalizable relationships
±2 day temporal matching window allows order-of-magnitude biomass change in rapidly developing blooms, introducing correlated label noise
Summer-only training and evaluation does not characterize performance during spring onset or fall decay, limiting operational usefulness
